--- SOURCE 2: Triangulation (social science) - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triangulation_(social_science)

SUMMARY:
Data triangulation: involves time, space, and persons . Uses multiple sources of data that all have a similar focus. Investigator triangulation: involves multiple researchers in an investigation. Theory triangulation: involves using more than one theoretical scheme in the interpretation of the phenomenon.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Triangulation (social science)

In the [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"), **triangulation** refers to the application and combination of several [research methods](/wiki/Research "Research") in the study of the same phenomenon.[[1]](#cite_note-1) By combining multiple observers, theories, methods, and [empirical](/wiki/Empirical_evidence "Empirical evidence") materials, researchers hope to overcome the weakness or intrinsic [biases](/wiki/Bias "Bias") and the problems that come from single method, single-observer, and single-theory studies.

It is popularly used in [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"). "The concept of triangulation is borrowed from [navigational](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") and [land surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") techniques that determine a single point in space with the convergence of measurements taken from two other distinct points."[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Triangulation can be used in both [quantitative](/wiki/Quantitative_research "Quantitative research") and [qualitative](/wiki/Qualitative_research "Qualitative research") studies as an alternative to traditional criteria like reliability and validity.

## Contents

## Purpose

The purpose of triangulation in qualitative research is to increase the credibility and validity of the results. Several scholars have aimed to define triangulation throughout the years.

## Types

Denzin (2006) identified four basic types of triangulation:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

--- SOURCE 3: What data counts in policymaking and programming evaluation ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150

SUMMARY:
As research strives to address novel 21st century issues including big data, pandemics, misinformation, and globalization, there is a need for rigorous social science and policy-based research to be aware of different interpretations of empirical data and valid research methods across disciplines, examine new and old phenomena using multi-method approaches, and validate data informing policy through methods of triangulation. In an ever-expanding world of big data and misinformation, there is a need for rigorous social science and policy-based research to identify phenomena and validate data sources through methods of triangulation. This research is based on a review of hundreds of top-cited articles found on academic search engines including _Google Scholar,_ applying the search terms related to research and triangulation theory, epistemology, and ontological perspectives relevant to social sciences, and policymaking.

FULL CONTENT:
What data counts in policymaking and programming evaluation – Relevant data sources for triangulation according to main epistemologies and philosophies within social science - ScienceDirect

What data counts in policymaking and programming evaluation – Relevant data sources for triangulation according to main epistemologies and philosophies within social science
=============================================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Jessi Hanson-DeFusco

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.evalprogplan.2023.102238](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.evalprogplan.2023.102238 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0149718923000150&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Triangulation validates interpretations of phenomena by testing data validity through convergence of evidence from various sources and methods. 
*   •Policy science is interdisciplinary yet discipline training of researchers often espouses specific epistemologies and science philosophies. 
*   •From logical positivism to hermeneutics, there are different social science interpretations of what data types qualify as empirical and reliable. 
*   •Rigorous research involves varied types of policy data and methods across disciplines, multimethod approaches, and triangulation validation. 

Abstract
--------

Policy analysis and program evaluation quality guides how impact is measured, revisions are made, and allocations of resources is deployed. As interdisciplinary research grows in contemporary policy science, the importance of defining truth can be contentious among different social scientists. This research traces the history of triangulation to its contemporary version within the social sciences. The study examines the philosophical evolution influencing the environment in which triangulation develops including: [consilience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/consilience "Learn more about consilience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the comparative [linguistic structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/structural-linguistics "Learn more about linguistic structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the traditions of thought; and the historical development of methods and emergence of triangulation in research. It also offers contrasting interpretations of triangulation within the various epistemologies and [philosophies of science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/philosophy-of-science "Learn more about philosophies of science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that have arisen in recent movements within social science. As research strives to address novel 21st century issues including [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), pandemics, misinformation, and globalization, there is a need for rigorous social science and policy-based research to be aware of different interpretations of empirical data and valid research methods across disciplines, examine new and old phenomena using multi-method approaches, and validate data informing policy through methods of triangulation.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000149)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718922001707)

Keywords
--------

Social science

Policy

Triangulation

Mixed methods

Epistemology

Philosophy

Consilience

Interpretation

Empirical data

1. Background
-------------

Policy and programming quality rely heavily on effective monitoring and evaluation. Evaluation methods are crucial to policymakers and implementers in determining best allocations of resources, prioritization, and the need for revisions. Policy analysis often requires multiple sources of data for evaluation, building on methods of triangulation. Yet what type of evaluation is conducted, what outcomes that they are searching for, how will the evaluation be carried out, by whom, with what sources of data are collected, and how they are analyzed can greatly differ among the various disciplines in policymaking and programming ([PEW, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib43), [Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib1)). As this research explores, the data that we seek often is tied to our specific training in the social sciences, all of which can have financial implications, define institutional protocols, as well as staffing.

In an ever-expanding world of [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and misinformation, there is a need for rigorous social science and policy-based research to identify phenomena and validate data sources through methods of triangulation. Triangulation involves using multiple methods and/or data sources in research to improve understanding of phenomena, as well as validate interpretations of the phenomena, testing the validity of data through the convergence of evidence from different sources ([Carter, Bryant-Lukosius, DiCenso, Blythe & Neville, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib13); Moon, 2019). However, what is perceived as _reliable_ and _empirical_ data frequently differs among the various disciplines in social science. Specifically, social science research often is interdisciplinary and multi-method ([Dunn, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib21), [Lasswell, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib34)), but its scholars tend to be more rigorously trained and self-identify with one or more disciplines, from economics, psychology, [public health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/public-health "Learn more about public health from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), to [political science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/political-science "Learn more about political science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Each discipline holds unique perspectives of what data qualifies as more scientifically-rigorous, empirical, and meaningful to interpreting meaning and confirming phenomena.

For instance, health scholarship and economic research tend to ascribe to large-N [quantitative studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/quantitative-study "Learn more about quantitative studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") collected from quasi-experimental and [experimental methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/experimental-method "Learn more about experimental methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), like randomized-control trials as producing _valid evidence_ ([Dunn, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib21), [Hausman and McPherson,](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib26), [O'Brien et al., 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib42), [Laska et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib33); Shadish, Cook, & Campbell, 2000). Additionally, contemporary health policy research assessing the impacts of Covid-19 focuses on producing and assessing quantitative data with large-scale collection including novel big [data analytics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/data-analytics "Learn more about data analytics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") like _data scrapping_. These forms of _quantitative pools of information_ are viewed as more robust, generalizable, and thus, more reliable and accurate, compared to qualitative sources or small-N studies ([Alsunaidi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib4), [Hart and Halden, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib25), [Poom et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib45)). Comparatively, while other disciplines like [environmental policy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/environmental-policy "Learn more about environmental policy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and digital anthropology often rely on big, empirical data, they also apply other data types including thick qualitative data as valid data points for triangulation ([Lu et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib36), [Somoza Sánchez et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib49)). Likewise, disciplines like education for decades heavily use a variety of data sources both qualitative and quantitative in triangulation, to validate research findings ([Kelle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib30), [Piccioli, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib44); Shadish et al.). Yet, a theoretical research gap remains between scholars from these various fields of which types of data really count as valid and reliable means of scientific evidence.

A historical analysis of the concept of _triangulation_ provides a unique reflection into the relationship between the diverse dominant epistemologies and [philosophies of science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/philosophy-of-science "Learn more about philosophies of science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the development of modern research methodology. Since the popularity of [logical positivism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/logical-positivism "Learn more about logical positivism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") through the next generations of falsificationism, hermeneutics, and pragmatism, both the definition and validation of truth-seeking have driven the consideration and redesign of accepted methods. The debate between theorists of these competitive traditions of thought over time expands the mapping of research potential for establishing empirical meaning in contemporary policy science. The purpose of triangulation relates to the scholarly pursuits to verify, falsify, confirm, or uncover. Yet these traditions of thought have discriminant, lending interpretations of triangulation which theoretically connect to the acceptance of _[consilience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/consilience "Learn more about consilience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_.

This research traces the evolution of triangulation to its contemporary version within the various disciplines of social sciences. The study further summarizes the philosophical backdrop that influenced the environment in which triangulation develops including: consilience and the [comparative linguistic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/comparative-linguistics "Learn more about comparative linguistic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") structure of the traditions of thought; and the historical development of methods and emergence of triangulation in research. This work examines its contrasting interpretations within the various epistemologies and philosophies of science that have arisen in recent movements within social science. Access to new data production methods like big data analytics continues to evolve along with more-readily availability of data at consumer [fingertips](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/fingertip "Learn more about fingertips from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). But ‘easier data’ can be a double-edged sword. In the 21st century, we are entering a world where information production cannot be easily distinguished as the binary- misinformation verses empirical data. There is a crucial need to better understand what counts as good data based on epistemological orientation, and how different disciplines’ theoretical definitions of empirical data can affect how we either validate scientific findings or identify validity issues.

2. Study purpose & method
-------------------------

This research is based on a review of hundreds of top-cited articles found on academic search engines including _Google Scholar,_ applying the search terms related to research and triangulation theory, epistemology, and ontological perspectives relevant to social sciences, and policymaking. This paper traces the evolution of triangulation to its contemporary version within the various disciplines of social sciences. The study further summarizes the philosophical backdrop that influenced the environment in which triangulation develops including: [consilience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/consilience "Learn more about consilience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the comparative [linguistic structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/structural-linguistics "Learn more about linguistic structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the traditions of thought; and the historical development of methods and emergence of triangulation in research. This work examines its contrasting interpretations within the various epistemologies and [philosophies of science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/philosophy-of-science "Learn more about philosophies of science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that have arisen in recent movements within social science. Access to new data production methods like [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analytics continues to evolve along with more-readily availability of data at consumer [fingertips](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/fingertip "Learn more about fingertips from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Policy analysis and program evaluation are increasingly diversifying the types of data used to inform practice. But ‘easier data’ can be a double-edged sword. In the 21st century, we are entering a world where information production cannot be easily distinguished as the binary- misinformation verses empirical data. There is a crucial need to better understand what counts as good data based on epistemological orientation, and how different disciplines’ theoretical definitions of empirical data can affect how we either validate scientific findings or identify validity issues.

3. Review of the literature
---------------------------

Policy and programming evaluations often require substantial resourcing. Cost and resourcing implications of evaluation often are predetermined by a) what outcomes that are being assessed, b) data collection design, c) the type of data being gathered, and d) the personnel taking part. The costs of evaluations will be heavier if examining multiple outcomes, complex outcomes, and a mixture of data sources ([PEW, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib43)). Yet too frequently, policy analysts and program evaluators do not consider the impacts that their training can have in 1) preparing them for data analysis, and 2) their personal biases about what data and data collection methods count. It can often led researchers to overlook different sources of data, data collection designs, and means of analysis. As policymaking is a multi-disciplinary science, policy analysis and program evaluation often merge various sciences together ([Dunn, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib20)), which may lead analysis teams to not always see eye-to-eye on what is effective, meaningful data.

Many institutions recognize the issue of their evaluation practices being inconsistent, weakly defined, and at times incomparable. In 2014, the Hewlett Foundation held a strategic roundtable to design and implemented “evaluation principles and practices” to better streamline evaluation decisions. The discussions that ensued highlighted the different opinions among its team members about what data should be prioritized (Twerkey & Arbreton, 2014). Additionally, as a response to the Trump administration’s cuts to US federal policy and programming budgeting, a senatorial bipartisan commission focused on evidence-based policymaking was launched to improve availability of administrative data for evaluation. Yet many state and federal agencies collect data inconsistently, using different methods, yet while the kinds of data may not be similar, they can be used for triangulating effectiveness from various evaluation lenses ([Feldman & Castleman, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib23)).

The evolvingly complex nature of scientific rigor in social science is rooted to logical positivist and falsification drives to abridge interpreted structural weaknesses in social science theoretical research by adopting scientific protocols of ‘harder’ sciences, like economics, statistics, and natural sciences ([Diesing, 1992](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib19), [Dunn, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib21)). In the pursuit to establish phenomena patterns and validate the concreteness of data, triangulation is increasingly applied in policy science like most social sciences. Generally, contemporary triangulation in social sciences is defined as a “technique that facilitates validation of data through cross verification from two or more sources. In particular, this concept refers to the application and combination of several research methods in the study of the same phenomenon” (Bodgan & Biklen, 2006, 5). Typically, its accepted use includes both quantitatively as a form of validation or verification, and qualitatively through inquiry. This technique is an established form of criteria to research quality synonymous to _creditability, reliability,_ and _validity_. However, there are different, specific representations of triangulation that are accepted among research theorists, depending on the researcher’s school of thought and discipline. A political economist may weight the validity of specific data types (e.g., information collected from an open-ended interview) as less articulate compared to a political scientist or policy-maker. Policy research that focuses primarily on qualitative data is less likely to be published in journals of [public health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/public-health "Learn more about public health from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or economics than if blended with a mixed-methods approach using an [RCT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/randomized-controlled-trial "Learn more about RCT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approach. The crux of which can have rippling effects among what evidence is accepted as accurate and reliable in public policy, a science of which is interdisciplinary and multiplicative in nature ([Dunn, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib21), 11).

Policy researchers tend to develop expertise in several social science fields, such as health, education, economics, or [political science](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/political-science "Learn more about political science from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Dunn). Policy stakeholders are often influenced by one or more epistemologies and philosophies of science historically informing the theoretical foundations of their primary and secondary professional concentrations. As such, they may by training be biased towards standardized notions of what is empirical data. As policy analysis increasingly threads various disciplines from economics to [public health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/public-health "Learn more about public health from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") together to create policy meaning, the kinds of data and research methods used to derive evidence may become politicized or misinterpreted among an ever-diversifying scientific audience. Each discipline in social science draws theoretically from specific epistemologies and philosophies. If not properly understood, information defined as rigorous in one science may be cast aside by another, thus limiting the variation of interpretation of meaning, and lowering the potential for policy analysis exploration.

Differing _traditions of thought_ (a synonym for epistemologies and philosophies of science) appear to incorporate methodological assumptions that allow for the notion of triangulation; although their individual constructs or interpretations often appear distinguishable. These interpretations of triangulation appear to be affected by gridding an epistemology or philosophy of science’s purpose towards truth and its definition of relevant science. This relationship is depicted in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#fig0005), implying a subjective mapping of various forms of epistemologies and philosophies of science along two axes: 1) pursuit of truth, and 2) foundations or rejection of metaphysics verses natural/hard sciences. These axes descend from the traditions’ doctrines and principles.

![Image 7: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0149718923000150-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (98KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0149718923000150-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (98KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0149718923000150-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Grid of epistemology & philosophy of sciences.

The interpretation of triangulation must incorporate the linguistics of multiple theoretical concepts. Firstly, the construct of triangulation depends on assumed paradigm represented within each philosophy or epistemology in the social sciences regarding use of multi-disciplinary and/or multi-research method. Moreover, the nature of triangulation also depends on the level by which a tradition of thought accepts or rebuts the existence and importance of metaphysical plane. Each tradition falls within one of these four quadrants of universal thought. Yet consilience can play a role in understanding how these concepts interact.

Consilience seeks to improve unification of different strands of knowledge to better the human condition ([Wilson, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib51), [Gillispie, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib22)). Consilience explores one set of facts of a theory through inductive analysis of facts obtained from another contrasting set acts as “a test of truth of the theory in which it occurs.” The establishment or dismissal of consilience may only occur through methods with relative foundations in the natural science. Yet, consilience extends beyond science through various paradigms of research to verify or confirm what is already known and the pursuit of learning the unknown through discovery and intellectual freedom. This language is similar to the characterized intended nature of policy utilized by [Lasswell (1999)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0149718923000150#bib34), who also emphasized the importance of multi-disciplinary perspectives in policy science (3−13). Wilson states that social issues are better solved through multiple disciplines.

Consilience blends the perspectives of different traditions of thought and a variety of disciplines, all of which relate but individually can stand apart. While the social sciences draw from the natural sciences in rooted methodological epistemology and philosophy, these sciences present a greater challenge to consilience as they seek to understand the human condition measurable to a set degree of certainty. The intersection of consensus of these traditions and disciplines can provide agreement on abstract principles and empirical evidence (Wilson). In this explanation, consilience provides an essential goal of triangulation. enriched_points.expanded_content: Triangulation enhances research reliability by integrating diverse data sources, allowing for comprehensive analysis and mitigating biases from single-method studies.
Utilizing multiple data sources and methods reinforces the validity of findings, ensuring that interpretations are robust and trustworthy across various social science disciplines.
Combining insights from various disciplines enriches understanding of phenomena, fostering a multi-dimensional approach that addresses complex social issues effectively.
